item.title: Lessons I Am Trying Not to Forget
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Not every post needs to be a framework.

Some posts are better as reminders.

This is one of those.

A few things I keep relearning, and apparently keep needing to relearn.

1) Clarity saves more energy than intensity​

When things get messy, people often respond with more urgency.

Sometimes what is actually needed is more clarity.

Intensity without clarity creates noise.

2) Smart people also need simple communication​

I used to think strong teams need more complexity because they can handle it.

Not always.

Strong teams move better when message is clean, not when it sounds sophisticated.

3) Some problems shrink when named properly​

A lot of stress comes from vague discomfort.

The moment you can say:

this is an ownership issue
this is a trust issue
this is a sequencing issue

things start becoming easier to deal with.

Naming does not solve the issue fully. But it reduces fog.

4) You can outgrow environments quietly​

Not with drama. Not with anger.

Sometimes a place simply stops stretching you. Or your values stop matching the way work is done.

That is useful to notice early.

5) What feels urgent is not always important​

This lesson keeps coming back forever.

Inbox urgency is seductive. Reaction feels productive.

But a lot of meaningful progress still comes from fewer, deeper, more deliberate moves.

6) Strong people also get tired in invisible ways​

This one is important.

A person can keep delivering and still be draining internally.

So not all burnout announces itself loudly. Sometimes it shows up as reduced joy, reduced sharpness, reduced care.

That needs attention too.

7) A good reputation is built in repeated ordinary moments​

Not only big wins.

It is built in:

  • how you respond under pressure
  • whether people can rely on your word
  • how you treat others when nothing is in it for you
  • how you handle disagreement

These ordinary moments compound.

Final thought​

I like big frameworks. But sometimes what helps most is a smaller list you can return to when life gets noisy.

This is one of those lists for me.

Maybe I will need to read it again later too.

item.title: Reclaiming 50GB+ Mac Storage Safely: A Journey from 'Disk Full' to AI-Powered Deduping
item.description: We've all been there: the dreaded "Your disk is almost full" notification on macOS. This week, I hit the wall. A 52GB folder of mobile backups, photos, and videos was choking my internal SSD.
item.content:encoded:
We've all been there: the dreaded "Your disk is almost full" notification on macOS. This week, I hit the wall. A 52GB folder of mobile backups, photos, and videos was choking my internal SSD.

But as an engineer, the thought of manually dragging-and-dropping thousands of files—or worse, manually deleting duplicates—felt like a recipe for disaster. One wrong click and a decade of memories could be gone.

Here is how I solved it using a "Safety-First" framework and a custom Python deduplication engine.

The Problem: Data Hoarding & The NTFS Wall​

I had a massive archive of media to move to my reliable Seagate Backup Plus. But I faced three major hurdles:

  1. The Risk of Loss: Drag-and-drop in Finder is "silent." If it fails halfway, you don't know what's copied and what's not.
  2. The NTFS Wall: My Seagate drive was formatted in NTFS (Windows standard), making it Read-Only on my Mac. I couldn't write a single byte to it.
  3. The Duplicate Nightmare: Over years of backups, I had hundreds of duplicate files with different names (e.g., IMG_123.jpg vs IMG_123_copy.jpg).

Attempt 1: The Manual Way (and why I didn't do it)​

Manual deletion is the enemy of accuracy. I considered online "Duplicate Finder" tools, but I didn't want to grant a random app access to my entire photo library, nor did I want to pay $20 for a one-time cleanup.

The Solution: AI-Guided "Safety-First" Migration​

I decided to pair-program the solution with my AI coding agent. We broke the process into 100% safe checkpoints.

Phase 1: Overcoming the NTFS Barrier​

Before moving data, I had to fix the "Read-Only" status. Guided by the AI, I installed a professional NTFS driver (Paragon). After a quick reboot and some permission juggling, my Seagate was finally writable.

Phase 2: The "Immutable" Copy (Rsync)​

Instead of a simple copy, we used rsync.

rsync -avh --progress --ignore-existing "[Source]" "[Destination]"

Why? Because rsync is resumable. If my Mac crashed during the 52GB transfer, I could just run it again, and it would pick up exactly where it left off, skipping everything already safe on the drive.

Phase 3: The "Fingerprinting" Logic (The 'Magic' Part)​

Once the data was safely on the Seagate, I wanted to remove duplicates. This is where the AI suggested something far superior to filename checking: SHA-256 Hashing.

The Logic Behind the Scenes:​

  1. Size Grouping: The script first groups files by their size. If two files are different sizes, they cannot be the same.
  2. Cryptographic Fingerprinting: For files with the same size, the script generates a unique SHA-256 "Hash" (a digital fingerprint).
  3. O(1) Lookup: Using a Python dictionary (Hashmap), we can check if we've seen that exact fingerprint before in constant time.

If the fingerprints match, the data is 100% identical byte-for-byte, regardless of the filename.

The Results​

  • Space Saved: 52GB instantly reclaimed on my Mac.
  • Accuracy: 100%. We verified the file counts and total bytes using find and du.
  • Time Invested: About 10 minutes of setup for a process that would have taken hours of manual sorting.
  • Cost: $0 ($20 saved on premium deduplication apps).

Conclusion​

This journey reminded me that coding isn't just for building products; it's a productivity superpower for our daily lives. By automating the "boring" and "risky" parts of file management with AI, I moved from a panicked "Disk Full" state to a perfectly organized, deduplicated archive in a single afternoon.

Safe moving, safe coding!

item.title: Memos I Wish More Leaders Wrote
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

I think many teams would become healthier if leaders wrote more honest memos.

Not longer memos. Not more polished memos.

Just more real ones.

So this post is in that spirit.

Below are 5 memos I wish more leaders wrote, or at least communicated this clearly.

Memo 1​

We are doing too many things.

So even though everyone is busy, progress feels thinner than it should.

This is not a motivation problem. It is a focus problem.

We need to choose fewer priorities and defend them harder.

Memo 2​

Some of our delays are not execution delays. They are decision delays.

We are waiting too long to clarify ownership, trade-offs, and direction.

That makes teams look slower than they really are.

Memo 3​

We say we want ownership, but sometimes we still punish people for acting without too much permission.

That contradiction confuses teams.

If we want ownership, then we also need to tolerate thoughtful initiative.

Memo 4​

Some meetings exist because trust is low.

That is uncomfortable, but true.

When leaders do not trust teams, they create extra layers of reviews, updates, and approvals.

Then everybody complains about bureaucracy.

Sometimes bureaucracy is just mistrust with calendar invites.

Memo 5​

A lot of what we call “leadership communication” is too abstract.

Teams do not only need inspiration. They also need plain clarity:

  • what matters most
  • what changed
  • what trade-off we made
  • what we are no longer prioritizing

That kind of clarity is respect.

Why I like memo-style thinking​

Because memos remove some theatre.

They force sharper thought.

You cannot hide weak thinking as easily when you have to write it clearly.

And maybe that is why I wish more leaders used them better.

Not to sound important. To reduce confusion.

Final thought​

If more leadership communication felt like a useful internal memo and less like a polished announcement, teams would probably trust it more.

And honestly, they would probably understand it better too.

item.title: Career Decisions Compound Quietly
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Most people notice career growth only when something visible happens.

Promotion. Big title. Big company. Comp jump.

But a lot of career trajectory gets shaped much earlier and much more quietly.

By decisions that do not look dramatic at the time.

That is why I think career decisions compound quietly.

And because they compound, small repeated choices matter more than people think.

What kind of choices am I talking about?​

Things like:

  • what kind of problems you choose
  • what kind of people you work with
  • whether you choose visibility or comfort
  • whether you build depth or only chase titles
  • whether you leave when learning stops

These choices dont always give instant reward.

But over years, they stack.

Compounding in career is strange​

In money, compounding is easier to notice.

In career, it is slower and less visible.

You may not see the value immediately when you:

  • choose a harder role
  • learn a harder system
  • work under a strong manager
  • stay with discomfort long enough to grow

But those things build judgment. And judgment compounds.

Later it starts showing up as:

  • better decisions
  • stronger opportunities
  • more trust
  • larger scope
  • clearer self-awareness

Short-term comfort can be expensive​

This is one of the hardest lessons.

Comfort is not always bad. But too much comfort creates hidden stagnation.

If a role gives:

  • predictable days
  • low challenge
  • no stretch
  • no new skill pressure

it may feel peaceful.

But if you stay there too long, the cost is not visible immediately. The cost appears later as reduced growth slope.

That is why some easy decisions become expensive only after years.

The people around you matter a lot​

This one compounds very strongly.

If you keep working with people who:

  • think clearly
  • raise your standards
  • challenge your assumptions
  • operate with ownership

then your own quality rises over time.

And if you spend years in low-ownership environments, that also leaves a mark.

People often underestimate this because it does not show up as bullet point on resume. But it absolutely shapes the person behind the resume.

Titles are useful, but not enough​

A lot of people optimize too directly for title.

I understand why. Titles matter.

But title without capability is fragile.

And capability built in the right environments tends to produce title anyway, eventually.

So I usually think the better question is:

am I becoming stronger in a way that compounds?

That includes:

  • judgment
  • communication
  • ownership
  • technical depth
  • leadership maturity

These are slow assets. But they travel well.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  1. choose learning slope over short-term comfort more often
  2. work with people who sharpen your standards
  3. optimize for capability, not only title
  4. notice where you are coasting too long
  5. remember that repeated small choices shape long-term trajectory

You do not need dramatic reinvention every year. Sometimes you just need better repeated decisions.

Final thought​

Career rarely changes only because of one big move.

Usually it changes because of dozens of smaller choices that looked ordinary when you made them.

That is what makes compounding so powerful.

It is quiet at first. Then one day people call it trajectory.

item.title: Executive Presence Without Theater
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Executive presence is one of those phrases that sounds useful and annoying at the same time.

Useful because it matters. Annoying because many people turn it into performance art.

I think the cleaner way to see it is this:

executive presence is not about sounding important, it is about creating confidence under pressure

That is very different.

What people usually get wrong​

They think executive presence means:

  • polished words
  • slow voice
  • serious expression
  • fancy slides
  • zero visible uncertainty

That is mostly theatre.

It may impress for few minutes. It does not hold up when situation becomes hard.

Real executive presence shows up more clearly when:

  • things are messy
  • leaders disagree
  • stakes are high
  • pressure is visible

Then people notice who brings clarity and calm.

Presence is really about signal quality​

When senior leaders speak, people are not only hearing content. They are reading signal.

Signal like:

  • do you understand the problem?
  • do you understand the trade-off?
  • can you simplify without oversimplifying?
  • can you stay steady without becoming robotic?

This is why some people sound senior even with simple language. And some people sound complicated without sounding trustworthy.

Calm is underrated​

Not fake calm. Real calm.

There is a big difference.

Fake calm hides uncertainty. Real calm holds uncertainty without spreading panic.

That matters a lot in leadership.

Because teams often borrow emotional signal from leaders.

If leader becomes scattered, urgency spreads badly. If leader becomes grounded, thinking improves.

Clarity matters more than polish​

I have seen leaders use sophisticated language and still leave room confused.

And I have seen others speak very simply and move entire room forward.

Why?

Because clarity beats polish.

At senior levels, people respect leaders who can say:

  • here is the real issue
  • here is what we know
  • here is what we dont know
  • here is the call we are making

That is presence.

Not vocabulary gymnastics.

Presence without substance collapses quickly​

This is important.

If someone has style but not judgment, it becomes visible over time.

Eventually people notice:

  • meetings sound strong but direction is fuzzy
  • communication is polished but decisions are weak
  • confidence is high but signal quality is low

So I dont think executive presence should ever be separated from real competence.

It is not a replacement. It is a carrier.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  1. simplify your message before trying to strengthen your tone
  2. do not hide uncertainty unnecessarily
  3. bring calm, especially when pressure is high
  4. say the actual trade-off clearly
  5. make people leave with more clarity than they came with

If that is happening regularly, presence is already improving.

Final thought​

Executive presence becomes powerful when it stops being performance.

For me, the best version of it is simple:

people trust your presence because your clarity, judgment, and steadiness make hard situations easier to navigate.

That is enough. No theatre needed.

item.title: The Cost of Being the Smartest Person in the Room
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Being smart is useful.

Building a room where only your intelligence matters is not.

A lot of leaders do this without realizing it.

They become the fastest thinker, quickest responder, final answer provider, and unofficial escalation endpoint for everything.

From outside, it looks impressive.

From inside, it creates dependency.

And dependency does not scale.

Why this happens​

Usually not because leader is arrogant.

Often because leader is capable.

They:

  • understand systems deeply
  • move faster than others
  • see mistakes early
  • know how to unblock things quickly

So naturally the team starts relying on them more.

Then slowly a pattern forms:

important things wait for one person.

That is where the trouble starts.

Intelligence can become a bottleneck​

This is the trap.

If your team cannot make strong decisions without you, then your intelligence is no longer only an asset. It has become a structural dependency.

That shows up in many ways:

  • meetings become approval sessions
  • senior people stop pushing their own view
  • execution slows when you are unavailable
  • team confidence becomes uneven

This is expensive.

Not just for speed. Also for leadership pipeline.

Strong leaders dont just solve​

They also create more solvers.

That is the harder job.

Anyone with enough experience can keep answering.

Much fewer people can build an environment where others start thinking with more clarity, more confidence, and more ownership.

That takes patience. And ego control.

How this shows up in meetings​

You can usually feel it quickly.

If leader speaks early and strongly, the room narrows.

People start optimizing for agreement. Not for thought.

Then later everybody says:

“yes makes sense”

and fewer people ask:

“wait, are we sure?”

That is not alignment. That is silent compression of thinking.

What good leaders do differently​

They do not make themselves small. They make more space for good thinking.

That can mean:

  • asking before answering
  • waiting before concluding
  • letting others own the frame
  • inviting disagreement early
  • rewarding clarity, not only confidence

This is subtle work. But powerful.

The hidden cost​

If leader becomes central brain of the team, three things happen:

  1. scale drops
  2. succession weakens
  3. team maturity stalls

That third one matters a lot.

Because people dont become strong by standing near strong leaders. They become strong by carrying meaningful judgment themselves.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  1. speak later in important discussions
  2. ask one more question before giving answer
  3. let capable people own uncomfortable decisions
  4. notice where your speed is suppressing others
  5. measure leadership by how much strong thinking exists without you

That last one is the real test.

Final thought​

There is no special prize for being the smartest person in the room if the room becomes weaker because of it.

At senior levels, the real win is different.

Can you raise the intelligence of the room itself?

That is much harder. That is also much more valuable.

item.title: Decision Making Under Ambiguity
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

A lot of people think senior leadership means having better answers.

Not exactly.

Many times it just means you have to decide before the answer becomes obvious.

That is the real difficulty.

Not decision making with full clarity. Decision making with partial information, limited time, strong opinions, and visible consequences.

That is why I think one of the most important leadership skills is this:

being able to move with imperfect information without becoming reckless.

Why this gets harder at senior levels​

At junior levels, many decisions are local.

At senior levels, decisions start carrying more weight:

  • org direction
  • hiring quality
  • architecture bets
  • platform priorities
  • reorgs
  • roadmap trade-offs

And the painful part is this:

you usually dont get all facts neatly arranged before making them.

If you wait for perfect certainty, you become slow. If you decide too fast, you become dangerous.

So the real game is not certainty. It is judgment.

Most bad decisions dont come from bad intent​

They usually come from one of these:

  • false urgency
  • ego attachment
  • too much optimism
  • weak challenge from others
  • fear of looking uncertain

This last one is huge.

Some leaders rush into confidence because they think uncertainty makes them look weak.

Actually the opposite is true.

Fake certainty is much more damaging than visible thoughtful uncertainty.

Good leaders ask better questions​

When things are ambiguous, I think these questions matter:

  • what do we know for sure?
  • what are we assuming?
  • what happens if we are wrong?
  • is this reversible or irreversible?
  • what is the cost of waiting?

That last one matters a lot.

Because sometimes delay looks safe, but is actually decision by avoidance.

Reversible vs irreversible decisions​

This is one of the cleanest frameworks.

Some decisions are reversible. You can adjust later.

Some are expensive to reverse. Those need more thought.

The mistake is treating all decisions with same weight.

Then you either:

  • over-analyze small things
  • under-think big things

Both are expensive.

Ambiguity is where leadership style gets exposed​

When path is clear, almost anyone can sound decisive.

But when:

  • data is incomplete
  • teams disagree
  • risks are real
  • pressure is rising

then leadership quality starts becoming visible.

Do you freeze? Do you panic? Do you overcontrol? Do you perform confidence? Or do you create enough clarity for people to move?

That is what people actually remember.

What helps in real life​

Practical version:

  1. separate facts from assumptions
  2. understand what can be reversed
  3. ask for challenge, not only alignment
  4. decide with conviction, but not ego attachment
  5. revisit decisions when new signal appears

This is important.

Changing your mind after new evidence is not weak leadership. It is healthy leadership.

Final thought​

Leadership is not tested most when things are obvious.

It is tested when things are unclear and people still need direction.

That is why decision making under ambiguity matters so much.

Not because leaders should pretend to know everything. But because teams need someone who can move thoughtfully even when the fog has not cleared yet.

item.title: What The Hard Thing About Hard Things Taught Me About Leading Through Pressure
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Some leadership books are clean.

This one is not.

That is exactly why The Hard Thing About Hard Things works.

It does not pretend leadership is all clarity, confidence, and vision slides.

It talks about the messy part:

  • uncertainty
  • pressure
  • bad options
  • layoffs
  • underperformance
  • morale damage
  • trying to act calm when you are not calm

That is real leadership. Not the LinkedIn version of it.

The biggest value of this book​

It normalizes something important:

sometimes there is no clean answer

Only hard trade-offs.

And once you lead large teams or business-critical systems, you see this often.

You may have to choose between:

  • speed and rework risk
  • loyalty and performance standards
  • short-term calm and long-term truth
  • keeping everyone comfortable and making necessary changes

That is the job.

Not because leadership is glamorous. Because reality is messy.

What stood out to me most​

1) There are times when things are just hard​

This sounds trivial. But it is actually powerful.

Many leaders waste energy asking:

“why is this so difficult?”

Because it is difficult. That is why leadership is being tested.

Sometimes the useful question is not “why is this happening to me?”

It is:

“what must I do clearly now?”

2) You cannot outsource hard conversations​

Underperformance. Misalignment. Broken trust. Org drift.

Leaders often delay these because they want one more week, one more signal, one more chance for things to self-correct.

Sometimes that patience is wise. Sometimes it is just avoidance wearing polite clothes.

This book is good at reminding you:

delayed truth usually becomes more expensive truth.

3) Peacetime leadership and wartime leadership are different​

I like this idea because it is practical.

Not every season needs same leadership style.

Sometimes the right move is:

  • broad input
  • exploration
  • talent development
  • patient decision making

And sometimes the right move is:

  • tight priorities
  • direct communication
  • fast escalation paths
  • zero ambiguity on ownership

The mistake is using one mode for every situation.

4) Culture is tested under pressure, not on posters​

Every company says it values transparency.

But what happens when:

  • targets are missed?
  • incidents pile up?
  • key people leave?
  • performance issue reaches senior level?

That is when culture becomes real.

Pressure reveals whether leadership values are actual operating principles or just decoration.

What this means for engineering leaders​

In engineering, especially in financial systems and platform teams, hard moments are unavoidable.

You will get:

  • production crises
  • leadership mismatch
  • org restructures
  • delivery slippage
  • strong people underperforming in bigger roles

And many of these do not have elegant answers.

So the real question becomes:

Can you stay clear without becoming cold? Can you stay honest without becoming destructive? Can you make hard decisions without becoming ego-driven?

That is serious leadership work.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  1. do not delay hard conversations unnecessarily
  2. name the situation clearly before trying to fix it
  3. adapt leadership mode to context
  4. separate discomfort from wrongness
  5. communicate more directly when stakes are high

None of this feels pleasant. But useful leadership is not always pleasant.

Final thought​

What I like about this book is that it respects reality.

It does not sell leadership as neat wisdom. It presents it as judgment under pressure.

And honestly, that feels much closer to what senior engineering leadership actually is.

Not always having the answer. But being willing to face the hard thing without pretending it is easy.

item.title: Team Topologies for Modern Financial and Platform Teams
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

A lot of engineering problems are not really architecture problems.

They look like architecture problems. They sound like architecture problems. They even get discussed in architecture meetings.

But many times the real issue is this:

team boundaries are wrong

That is why Team Topologies is such a useful book.

It helps you see something many engineering leaders learn the hard way:

bad org design creates bad software behavior.

Why this book matters​

When systems get bigger, complexity doesnt only live in code. It also lives in:

  • ownership confusion
  • dependency chains
  • platform bottlenecks
  • too many handoffs
  • teams carrying too much cognitive load

And if you dont design around these, adding more people does not make system better. It just makes confusion scale faster.

The idea I like most: cognitive load is real​

This is the part many leaders ignore.

A team can only hold so much in its head:

  • business rules
  • infra knowledge
  • deployment complexity
  • security expectations
  • stakeholder needs
  • operational ownership

After some point, the team is not lazy. The team is overloaded.

And overloaded teams do predictable things:

  • avoid change
  • copy old patterns
  • make safe but mediocre decisions
  • move slower than they should

So when a team looks “slow”, I always think this first:

Are they weak? Or are they carrying too much?

The 4 team types are useful because they force clarity​

The book talks about clear team patterns.

I wont repeat whole framework here. But the practical value is this:

not every team should be doing everything.

Some teams should own product flow. Some should reduce platform friction. Some should enable others temporarily. Some should handle specialized expertise.

This sounds obvious. But many companies build teams like this instead:

“you people do product, infra, reliability, compliance support, and data workflows also please”

That is not empowerment. That is chaos with Jira access.

Platform teams are often misunderstood​

A platform team is not valuable because it exists.

It is valuable only if it reduces friction for other teams.

If platform team creates:

  • tickets for everything
  • slow approvals
  • complicated onboarding
  • too much internal documentation with little usability

then platform has become another dependency tax.

Good platform teams feel like force multipliers.

Developers should feel:

“nice, this made my work easier”

not

“great, one more internal system to fight with”

Team interaction is where things get interesting​

This is another strong point in the book.

Leaders often focus only on team structure. But interaction model matters just as much.

How do teams work together?

  • are they collaborating too much for too long?
  • are they blocked on a platform team?
  • are they unclear on service ownership?
  • are they depending on tribal knowledge?

Because if interaction mode is messy, structure alone wont save you.

What this means in financial systems and enterprise platforms​

In banks, fintechs, and enterprise engineering, this becomes even more important.

Because now you also have:

  • compliance
  • security constraints
  • change governance
  • resilience expectations
  • many cross-functional stakeholders

So if team boundaries are fuzzy, everything becomes slower.

And then people say:

“enterprise is slow”

Sometimes yes. But sometimes org design is just poor.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  1. check if teams own clear outcomes, not vague components
  2. reduce cognitive load before blaming execution
  3. make platform teams usability-focused
  4. review handoffs like you review architecture
  5. redesign boundaries when dependency pain becomes chronic

This is not HR work. This is system design.

Final thought​

Software architecture and org architecture keep leaking into each other.

You cannot fix one forever while ignoring the other.

That is why I like Team Topologies.

It reminds engineering leaders that scaling is not just about better code.

Sometimes it is about putting the right team around the right problem in the right way.

item.title: Why the Best Engineering Leaders Act Like Multipliers, Not Bottlenecks
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Some leaders enter a room and make everyone sharper.

Some leaders enter a room and make everyone quieter.

That is the simplest summary of Multipliers.

The book is basically asking:

Are you making people think better? Or are you making them wait for your answer?

This sounds simple. But in engineering leadership it is a huge difference.

The leadership trap​

A lot of capable leaders become accidental diminishers.

Not because they are bad. Because they are smart, fast, experienced, and used to solving hard problems.

So what happens?

They:

  • answer too quickly
  • jump into every hard decision
  • rewrite other people’s thinking
  • over-direct experienced engineers
  • treat meetings like approval gates

From outside it looks like leadership.

Inside the team, it feels like this:

“why should I think deeply if final answer is already decided?”

That is how strong teams become passive.

Why this matters in engineering​

Good engineering orgs need local decision quality.

They cannot depend on one smart person for:

  • architecture direction
  • delivery rescue
  • incident decisions
  • stakeholder translation
  • talent growth

That model breaks at scale.

And to be honest, it becomes exhausting too.

What a multiplier does differently​

1) Gives ownership, not just tasks​

Task is: “go implement this”

Ownership is: “understand the problem, make trade-offs, and bring me the best path”

Huge difference.

The first creates executors. The second creates leaders.

2) Uses questions well​

Not fake questions. Not interrogation.

Real questions like:

  • what problem are we actually solving?
  • what assumption are we making?
  • what fails if traffic doubles?
  • where is the operational risk?

Good questions dont slow the team. They improve thinking.

3) Creates safe intellectual pressure​

I like this idea a lot.

Best teams are not relaxed because nobody cares. They are sharp because standards are high and thinking is expected.

So multiplier leadership is not soft.

It means:

  • high bar
  • strong ownership
  • low ego

That combination is rare.

What engineering leaders should watch out for​

There are some signs that you may be becoming bottleneck:

  1. important decisions keep waiting for you
  2. meetings become updates for your approval
  3. senior people stop disagreeing openly
  4. team quality drops when you step away
  5. escalation volume stays high even after team growth

These are not random signals. They usually mean leverage is weak.

My biggest takeaway​

As leaders grow, temptation grows too.

Because you know more. You have seen failures. You can often predict the answer.

But if you always provide answer, you may be solving today’s problem by weakening tomorrow’s org.

This is where multiplier mindset becomes important.

Sometimes the job is not to be the smartest voice in the room.

Sometimes the job is to make the room smarter.

What I would apply​

Practical version:

  • ask one more good question before giving answer
  • delegate outcomes, not just tasks
  • let senior engineers own trade-offs publicly
  • reward clear thinking, not only fast execution
  • check whether your presence improves energy or suppresses it

That last one is painful but useful.

Final thought​

The best engineering leaders are not only good problem solvers.

They are force multipliers for judgment, clarity, and confidence across the team.

If team can only be strong when leader is fully involved, leader is not scaling yet.

item.title: A Note on Trust
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

This one is not a framework post.

Just a note.

The longer I work with people and teams, the more I feel this:

trust is not a soft concept sitting outside execution

It is part of execution.

Maybe the most invisible part.

What trust changes​

When trust is high:

people speak earlier
issues surface faster
disagreement becomes useful
ownership becomes lighter, not heavier

When trust is low:

people protect themselves
updates become political
feedback becomes late
meetings become performance

Same org chart. Very different energy.

Trust is built in small moments​

Not big speeches.

Small moments like:

  • do you react badly to bad news?
  • do you keep your word on small things?
  • do people feel safe disagreeing with you?
  • do you give context or just authority?
  • do you stay fair when stressed?

This is where trust grows. Or quietly dies.

One thing I keep noticing​

People say they want honesty.

But many environments punish honesty indirectly.

The person who raises risk gets labeled difficult. The person who asks hard question gets seen as negative. The person who surfaces problem early feels exposed.

Then later everyone asks:

why didnt anybody say this before?

Usually because system trained them not to.

Trust and speed are connected​

This is why I dont see trust as separate from performance.

High trust teams move faster because:

  • they escalate sooner
  • they hide less
  • they ask for help earlier
  • they spend less energy managing perception

That is real speed.

Not rushed motion. Cleaner motion.

A small personal view​

I think trust is one of those things people notice more in absence than in presence.

When it is there, work feels smoother. When it is missing, everything becomes heavier than it should be.

Conversations become careful. Energy becomes fragmented. Even good people start operating below their best.

Final thought​

A lot of leadership advice talks about strategy, output, visibility, influence.

All useful.

But under many of those things, trust is quietly doing the real work.

And if trust goes weak, everything else starts costing more.

item.title: What Accelerate Means for Leaders Building High-Trust Engineering Teams
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Many teams say:

we want speed
we want quality
we want stability

Then they behave as if they can pick only one.

That is exactly why Accelerate is useful.

Because it challenges one of the most common engineering myths:

speed and stability are not enemies by default

Bad systems make them enemies. Good systems improve both together.

What I like about this book​

It is not based on “my experience says”.

It is based on patterns observed across many teams.

That matters.

Because engineering leaders often fall in love with opinions:

  • “more process means control”
  • “change freezes improve reliability”
  • “large releases are safer because they are planned more”

All of these can sound smart in meetings. But in practice, many times they create slow and fragile systems.

4 ideas that matter a lot​

1) Deployment frequency matters​

If releasing is painful, risky, political, and rare, your team is already paying tax.

Why?

Because rare releases create:

  • bigger batch sizes
  • slower feedback
  • more fear
  • harder rollback

Smaller, repeatable change is usually healthier.

In simple language:

shipping more often is not chaos
shipping unpredictably is chaos

2) Lead time is a truth-teller​

How long does it take for an idea or code change to reach production?

That number exposes many hidden problems:

  • unclear ownership
  • weak review process
  • slow test pipelines
  • approval theatre
  • architecture dependency mess

When lead time is bad, culture is usually not the only issue. System design and process design are also shouting.

3) Reliability is not achieved by fear​

Some orgs try to achieve stability by making everyone scared of change.

That creates:

  • slow releases
  • hidden risk
  • delayed fixes
  • blame-heavy culture

Reliable teams are not the teams that avoid change. They are teams that can make change safely.

Big difference.

4) Culture is not “soft”​

This is where I strongly agree with the book.

When teams have trust:

  • issues are raised earlier
  • code reviews get better
  • incidents become learning loops
  • people dont waste energy on defensive politics

So yes, culture is a delivery topic. Not HR wallpaper.

What this means for engineering leaders​

If you are leading a fintech, bank platform, or any serious engineering org, you cannot just ask:

“Why is delivery slow?”

You should ask:

  • how hard is it to release?
  • how long does change take?
  • how often do we recover from failure?
  • what in our system creates fear?

Because output problems are often system problems.

Things I would actually do​

After reading Accelerate, practical actions become clearer:

  1. reduce batch sizes
  2. improve test confidence
  3. shorten approval chains
  4. make incidents learning-oriented
  5. measure flow, not only effort

And yes, dashboards help. But only if they lead to structural improvement.

Vanity metrics are still vanity metrics.

One mistake leaders make​

They hear “move fast” and translate it into:

do more work
push harder
reduce discussion

That is not what high performance means.

High performance means reducing friction in the system so good work moves faster with less drama.

Final thought​

For me, Accelerate is really about this:

trust, engineering discipline, and delivery performance are deeply connected

If a team has to choose between speed and stability all the time, that is usually not a people problem.

That is architecture, process, and leadership asking for attention.

item.title: What Good Leaders Repeat
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Some leaders love saying new things.

Good for conferences. Not always good for teams.

Because in actual teams, one of the most underrated leadership skills is not originality.

It is useful repetition.

The best leaders I have seen repeat the right things so consistently that people stop feeling confused even when work is hard.

Why repetition matters​

Teams forget.

Not because they are careless. Because they are busy.

People are dealing with:

  • sprint pressure
  • roadmap changes
  • production issues
  • cross-team dependencies
  • personal stress

So if a leader says something important once and assumes everyone “got it”, that is usually fantasy.

Good leadership often sounds repetitive from the speaker side and clarifying from the team side.

What should be repeated?​

Not everything.

Only the few things that create stability.

Like:

  • what matters most right now
  • what good quality looks like
  • what trade-offs we are willing to make
  • how we make decisions
  • what behaviors are non-negotiable

This kind of repetition builds operating rhythm.

Without it, teams start making up their own story. And that is where misalignment quietly begins.

Repetition is not lack of depth​

Actually it is usually the opposite.

If something is truly important, it deserves repeated attention.

Vision needs repetition. Standards need repetition. Values under pressure definitely need repetition.

People do not remember what leader said once in a perfect offsite slide.

They remember what leader kept reinforcing when reality got messy.

The test is simple​

If I ask 5 people in a team:

  • what matters most this quarter?
  • how do we decide priorities?
  • what does quality mean here?

and I get 5 different answers, then leader has not communicated enough.

Or not clearly enough.

Repetition also creates trust​

When a leader keeps changing tone, priorities, and message every week, teams become cautious.

Not because they are resistant. Because uncertainty increases.

But when leaders repeat the right things consistently, teams start feeling:

okay, I know what this person stands for I know what matters here I know what will not randomly change tomorrow

That predictability is calming. And calm teams think better.

Things worth repeating in leadership​

If I had to pick a few:

  1. what problem we are actually solving
  2. where quality bar sits
  3. how decisions get made
  4. what kind of behavior is rewarded
  5. what trade-offs we are not willing to make

This sounds basic. It is also where a lot of leadership strength lives.

Final thought​

A lot of leaders try too hard to sound fresh.

I think a better question is:

what is so important that it deserves to be heard again?

That is often where the real leadership message is hiding.

item.title: What High Output Management Still Gets Right for Engineering Leaders
item.content:encoded:
Read This​

Most people think leadership means:

more meetings
more escalations
more approvals
more visibility

Nope.

Leadership at scale is mostly about one thing:

Can your team produce good output without you becoming the bottleneck?

That is why I still like High Output Management.

It is an old book, yes. But old doesnt mean outdated.

Some books teach motivation. This one teaches operating system.

And if you are leading engineering teams in fintech, banking, or any high pressure setup, operating system matters a lot more than motivational quotes.

What the book gets very right​

1) Manager output is not just your output​

This is the biggest thing.

As a manager, your output is:

  • what your team ships
  • how your team makes decisions
  • how fast your team learns
  • whether your team can operate when you are not in the room

That sounds obvious. But many engineering leaders still behave like senior individual contributors with calendar access.

They solve the hardest issue themselves. They review every important PR. They attend every architecture meeting. They become the reliability layer for the entire org.

It works for some time. Then the team slows down.

Why?

Because leader became middleware.

2) Meetings are not automatically waste​

People love saying:

this meeting should have been an email

Sometimes true. But all meetings are not waste.

A good meeting is a production system for clarity.

  1. 1:1s reduce hidden issues
  2. staff meetings align teams
  3. decision reviews prevent rework
  4. planning meetings expose assumptions early

Bad meeting = status theatre
Good meeting = decision acceleration

In high-scale engineering orgs, confusion is more expensive than meetings.

3) Task relevant maturity is real​

This idea is underrated.

Same person can be:

  • highly mature in system design
  • medium mature in stakeholder handling
  • low mature in org leadership

So managing everyone with one style is lazy.

Some people need direction. Some need context. Some need challenge. Some just need you to stop interfering.

That is real leadership.

Not treating everyone same. But treating everyone fairly based on what helps them grow and deliver.

4) Leverage is the real game​

Good leaders create leverage through:

  • hiring well
  • coaching well
  • setting standards
  • improving decision quality
  • removing repeated friction

Example:

If you solve one production issue yourself, good.

If you improve design review quality so 20 such issues dont happen, much better.

If you create stronger engineering managers who can do that without you, even better.

This is how senior leadership starts looking different from just “working hard”.

What I take from it as an engineering leader​

For me this book is not about “how to manage”.

It is about how to avoid becoming accidental bottleneck while thinking you are helping.

Especially in financial systems, payments, platform teams, or enterprise engineering:

  • complexity is high
  • dependencies are real
  • stakes are high
  • speed still matters

So leadership cannot just be inspiration. It has to be operational.

What I would actually apply​

If I reduce the whole book into practical points:

  1. define output clearly
  2. create repeatable operating rhythm
  3. invest in 1:1s seriously
  4. calibrate people based on task maturity, not title
  5. measure whether team quality improves without your constant intervention

If that last one is not happening, something is wrong.

Final thought​

High Output Management is still relevant because engineering leadership is still same in one important way:

the higher you go, the less your value comes from doing the work yourself.

Your real value comes from building a system where good work keeps happening.

That is hard. That is also the job.

item.title: Laws Of Human Nature
item.description: 1. LAW OF IRRATIONALITY: Master Your Emotional Self
item.content:encoded:
1. LAW OF IRRATIONALITY: Master Your Emotional Self

    Humans think that we’re rational and in control of our lives, when in reality we’re driven by irrational emotions. We’re often guided by our emotional impulses—we seek pleasure, avoid pain and do things to soothe our ego.

    Emotions and conscious thought are located in different parts of the brain—we first feel an emotion before we interpret it consciously (and often wrongly). It’s easier to think that you’re angry with someone because he’s a jerk, than to admit that you’re insecure and jealous of him. In short, we’re emotional creatures, yet we’re often blind to our own impulses. Become aware of your own irrationality, then train yourself to learn and manage them through introspection and reflection.

    Digest these powerful ideas in minutes with our full summary & infographic!

    BECOMING MORE RATIONAL To become more rational, incorporate these 3 steps into your life:

• Recognize biases (or “low-grade irrationality”) that distort your perception and responses. These include the (i) confirmation bias (thinking you’re being logical when you’re focusing on evidence to confirm your own beliefs), (ii) conviction bias (defending your views with conviction to drown out your doubts), (iii) appearance bias (thinking you can read people when you’re misled by them), (iv) group bias (thinking your ideas are original when you’re following the herd), (v) superiority bias (thinking you’re better and more rational than others) and (vi) blame bias (avoiding your own failures and blaming others).

• Beware of inflaming factors (or “high-grade irrationality”) which call up certain feelings and intensify them. These may come from (i) childhood memories that trigger strong positive/negative feelings, (ii) sudden gains/losses that bring extreme optimism or pessimism, (iii) extreme stress which causes us to get defensive, (iv) individuals who evoke strong feelings in us/others, and (v) infectious emotions from large groups. Watch out for these factors, detach yourself and contemplate the underlying sources.

• Develop your rational self: (i) observe yourself in times of stress, (ii) examine the roots of your feelings and record your observations, (iii) wait before you respond, (iv) accept irrational people as facts of life (vs taking things personally), and (iv) consciously channel your emotional energy instead of being driven by them.

  1. LAW OF NARCISSISM: Transform Self-Love into Empathy Humans possess the natural ability to empathize and connect with others. Unfortunately, we get blinded by self-absorption. Our sense of self-worth is tied to the attention we receive and the quality of our interactions. The problem arises when we rely on others’ attention and validation to feel worthy and alive.

We are all narcissists to varying degrees. Deep narcissists lack a cohesive sense of self, so their self-worth comes entirely from others’ attention and validation. They tend to take everything personally, demand control and attention in their relationships and are expert manipulators as leaders. Most of us are functional narcissists in the middle of the spectrum. We may slip into moments of self-absorption, but have a strong-enough sense of self to pull out of it. Healthy narcissists have a strong, resilient sense of self. They direct their sensitivity outward—in the form of empathy toward people or focused creativity in their work—instead of inward.

SHIFT TOWARD HEALTHY NARCISSISM Aim to (i) recognize deep narcissism to avoid being sucked into toxic relationships, (ii) be honest about your true nature, and (iii) shift toward healthy narcissism by developing 4 empathic skills:

• Empathetic attitude: Assume you’re ignorant about people and be truly curious about their point of view. In your daily conversations, (i) set aside your judgments/biases, (ii) stop your inner dialogue and listen fully, (iii) don’t assume you have similar/shared views, and (iv) be open to new discoveries.

• Visceral empathy: Pay attention to people’s reactions, e.g. facial expressions, body language and tone of voice. Use mirroring to develop a stronger connection, e.g. mimic their tone or expression, nod and smile as you listen.

• Analytic empathy: gather info about the other person to know them (e.g. family relationships, values, emotional triggers).

• Use direct/indirect feedback to develop your empathetic skills: ask about their thoughts/feelings to verify your guesses, and observe their responses and rapport.

  1. Law of Role Playing: See though people’s masks. Become a master at reading people and presenting yourself optimally with 3 skills.

  2. Law of Compulsive Behavior: Know people’s character. When choosing who to work/associate with, learn to look beyond appearances. Learn to assess your own character (so you can address the negative patterns in your life) and becoming skilled at reading others’ character.

  3. Law of Covetousness: Become an elusive object of desire. It’s human tendency to covet what we don’t have. Learn and apply the 3 strategies for stimulating desire.

  4. Law of Shortsightedness: Elevate your perspective. Learn to step back and consider the bigger picture instead of reacting to what’s in front of you. Recognize and overcome the 4 signs of shortsightedness.

  5. Law of Defensiveness: Confirm people’s self-opinion. To influence someone, don’t try to show how good you are nor challenge them directly. Instead, use 5 strategies to soften their defenses (by validating their self-opinion and aligning your idea with it).

  6. Law of Self-sabotage: Your attitude shapes your situation. Our attitude affects how we interpret and respond to events/people, thus creating a self-fulfilling effect. Overcome 5 constricted attitudes and embrace expansive ones, to improve your circumstances.

  7. Law of Repression: Confront your dark side. We all have a dark side (e.g. the selfish, greedy, or aggressive parts of ourselves) that we hide in order to fit in and be liked/respected. Recognize the 7 common emphatic traits that hide a darker side, and use 4 steps to tap on your full range of creative energies and become an integrated human.

  8. Law of Envy: Beware the fragile ego. Humans constantly compare ourselves with one another, and deny our own envy/jealousy. Learn to decode and diffuse envy before it turns dangerous. Learn the signs of envy, the 5 envier types, the situations that often trigger active envy, and how to build your self-worth.

  9. Law of Grandiosity: Know your limits. Humans want to think highly of ourselves, to the point we tend to imagine our superiority. Learn to identify the signs of elevated grandiosity in yourself and in others, including the 6 common illusions of grandiose leaders and how you can adopt practical grandiosity.

  10. Law of Gender Rigidity: Reconnect to your masculinity/femininity. Every human has both masculine and feminine qualities genes, and hormones. Learn to recognize 6 types of gender projections and unlock your repressed qualities/energy to become more flexible, balanced and effective.

  11. Law of Aimlessness: Advance with a sense of purpose. Use 5 strategies to overcome in-the-moment impulses to develop a sense of purpose and follow your inner compass.

  12. Law of Conformity: Resist the groups’ downward pull. We like to believe that we’re independent and progressive, but we can’t help conforming with our groups. Develop group intelligence by knowing how you’re individually influenced by groups and recognize the dynamics/patterns found in any group, then use 5 strategies to develop healthy groups with an upward pull.

  13. Law of Fickleness: Make them want to follow you. We want to believe that our emotions are pure and simple, when we’re actually ambivalent toward most things, including our leaders. Learn to establish your authority with 8 strategies and cultivate your inner authority.

  14. Law of Aggression: See the hostility behind the façade. On the surface, people seem friendly and civilized. But beneath the mask, everyone has an aggressive side. Learn to recognize and manage chronic aggressors, counter passive-aggression, be aware of your own aggressive tendencies, and harness 4 positive aspects of your assertive energy.

  15. Law of Generational Myopia: Seize the historical moment. We’re strongly defined by the generation that we’re born into. Realize how history moves in cycles across 4 generations, understand how your generation’s spirit affects you, and how/where you fit in the wider generational patterns. Then, use several strategies to exploit the spirit of the times.

  16. Law of Death Denial: Contemplate common mortality. Instead of avoiding thoughts of death, leverage the paradoxical death effect–use the awareness of your mortality to make your life more productive and meaningful.

SO these were the 18 laws. Entire content has been copied from internet, as it has been written beautifully @readingraphics.

item.title: Chakras
item.content:encoded:
Know your chakras - ( session from Intellectual Indies youtube)

1) Root chakra ( Muladhar Chakra)​

Base of spine , red energy emmision​

  • sceintific explanation - it is adrenaline gland
  • people who are low on energy and who doesnt feel like doing anything or getting up in the morning have less adrenaline hormones
  • excercise helps to keep it active
  • emits engery and attracts people
  • make your frnd circle full of engery to enhance this chakra

2) Sakral chakra ( Swadishtan Chakra)​

Above root chakra , orange in color​

  • it controls emotions when to get angry, happy etc
  • sceintific explanation - endocrine glands
  • it's like magnetism , if people get attracted to you then this is your active chakra If this chakra is:
  • open - joyful, Adventurous
  • close - alone, limited frnd circle, fixed routine, think negative of themselves way to open this:
  • make happiness a priority

3) Solar Plexus chakra ( Manipur Chakra)​

Around navel in abdomen, yellow color​

  • will power, successful
  • we need to increase will power to enhance this chakra
  • keep small tasks and complete them to increase will power

4) Heart Chakra ( Hridaya Chakra)​

Center of chest, green color​

  • thymus gland
  • close body indicates that this chakra is closed
  • when it's powerful - you see everyone with love
  • forgive everyone to enhance this chakra
  • close - cold hand , like lonliness
  • meditation is helpful for this
  • gratitude practice, write 3 reasons why you are happy
  • when you gratitude, you attract success

5) Throat Chakra ( Vishuddhi Chakra)​

Throat, blue color, thyroid gland, element- ether​

  • excessive speaking - extra open
  • share knowledge - helps to strengthen chkra
  • write journal - say truth, write truth to enhance this
  • low volume - means this chakra is close

6) Third eye chakra ( Agya Chakra)​

Forehead, Pituatiory gland, pineal gland, Ivory color​

  • knowledge chakra
  • education seeker, intuition
  • observing people, deep thinker

7) Crown Chakra ( Sahastrasaar Chakra)​

Top of head, violet royal blue color​

  • Old kings used to wear violet color gowns as they knew it
  • Penial Gland ( antenna ) center of brain
  • englightment
  • regulate bmt harmone
  • beleive in universe
  • down - if you feel nothing is working for you
  • try to read universal interpretations to enhance it

item.title: The Backwards Law: Why Trying Harder Doesn't Always Work
item.description: The Backwards Law: The Art of Letting Go
item.content:encoded:
The Backwards Law: The Art of Letting Go​

The "Backwards Law," a term famously explored by philosopher Alan Watts, posits a counter-intuitive truth: The harder you try to get a result, the further away it moves.

The Paradox of Desire​

Alan Watts was a British writer who translated Eastern philosophy for the Western world. He famously wrote:

"Wanting a positive experience is a negative experience; accepting a negative experience is a positive experience."

This is the core of the Backwards Law. When you pursue happiness, you are reinforcing the fact that you lack it in the first place. This constant chase makes you less satisfied.

Real-Life Examples:​

  1. Sleep: The more you worry about falling asleep, the more awake you stay.
  2. Social Ease: The harder you try to "look cool" or "act natural," the more awkward you become.
  3. Confidence: The truly confident person doesn't need to feel confident; they are simply okay with whatever happens.

Conclusion: Sometimes, stepping back and accepting the current situation—even if it's negative—is the fastest way to improve it. In many situations, struggling only makes the water murkier.

#learnings

item.title: Rich Dad's Guide to Investing: Building Lasting Wealth
item.description: Key Principles from Rich Dad's Guide to Investing
item.content:encoded:
Key Principles from Rich Dad's Guide to Investing​

Robert Kiyosaki’s guide isn't about particular stocks; it's about the Mindset of an investor. Here is the distilled wisdom on building an investment-first lifestyle:

1. Distinguish Good vs. Bad "Investments"​

Kiyosaki warns against using your capital for "liabilities" disguised as lifestyle upgrades.

  • Bad Moves: Quitting a steady job for a "world tour" without passive income, or liquidating family stability for shallow desires.
  • Asset-First: Every profit should be reinvested. The goal is to maximize the gap between your income and your living expenses by using that gap to buy more assets.

2. The 3 E's of an Investor​

To be a successful investor, you must acquire these three "E's":

  1. Education: Financial literacy is your #1 asset. Investment without knowledge is just gambling.
  2. Experience: Start small. You need the "street smarts" of seeing how numbers move in real-time.
  3. Excess Cash: Never let cash sit idle. Use it to acquire more "meaningful work" (like hiring employees for monotonous tasks) so your own time is freed up for high-level strategy.

3. Investment as a Knowledge Compound​

Knowledge is a unique asset because it compounds faster than money. By investing in yourself—through books, seminars, and networking—you become an "Inside Investor."

The Golden Rule: Never buy a stock just to sell it. Buy the company because you believe in its value as if it were your own business.

#learnings

item.title: Own It: What Maturity Actually Means
item.description: What Does it Really Mean to be Mature?
item.content:encoded:
What Does it Really Mean to be Mature?​

Most people define maturity by age, but real maturity is binary: It is the ability to own your actions (and even others' mistakes) to find a solution rather than a target for blame.

The Lesson of the Croma Cooler​

I recently had a frustrating experience with an online order. I needed a replacement for a faulty air cooler. When the pickup agent was rude and refused to take the unboxed unit, I reacted predictably: I matched his tone, defended my stance, and slammed the door.

Result: A week passed, and I was still stuck with a broken cooler and a heated mood.

If I had shown maturity—owning the outcome (getting the cooler replaced) instead of the ego-clash—I could have handled it differently, stayed calm, and resolved the issue that same day.

10 Pillars of Maturity​

To be mature is to master these habits:

  1. Take Responsibility: Don't just own your successes; own your blockers.
  2. No Complaints: Focus on "What's next?" instead of "Why me?"
  3. Stop Gossiping: Small minds discuss people; mature minds discuss ideas.
  4. Self-Awareness: List your own weaknesses. Knowing them is the first step to neutralizing them.
  5. Leave Ego Behind: Ego is a barrier to learning.
  6. Seek Value, Not Attention: Do great work, and the praise will find you.
  7. Total Honesty: Small lies build a weak foundation.
  8. The Emotional/Rational Balance: Feel your emotions, but let your rational mind lead.
  9. Calm Response to Rejection: Rejection is just data, not a personal attack.
  10. Stay Curious: Maturity isn't "knowing everything." Keep the child within alive by asking questions.

#learnings

item.title: Primal Learning - How Brain learns
item.description: Mylan coating around brain cells help you retrieve faster
item.content:encoded:
Mylan coating around brain cells help you retrieve faster

Use it or lose it​

Implicit:

You learn more per hour if you space it out

You become average of the 5 people you spend most time with

Explicit:

Exercise and Meditation helps

Do important things first

Keep a distraction log- remember what distract you - becoming aware of what distracts you

Reference memory:

Green tea black tea and breath- alpha memory ( concentration )

Theta memory - 2 to 5years child - extraordinary learning

Set goal regular for dopamine release which will help you learn

Goals: subgoals Deadlines: many nd short

Each deadline you miss if you

Cold showers it Increase dopamine level by 250% and 500% noradrenaline

Vary exercise and push your limits for great endorphins release

Start singletasking

A day without setting goal is a day when you are not operating your best

  1. struggle phase

Find out what you are afraid of and create strong emotional attachment

  1. start easy
  2. reward yourself for small goals
  3. start moving, get up and walk around , moving around motion create emotion
  4. create deadline
  5. get accountability partner
  6. get mad at fear Cortisol + noradrenaline they create uneasy feeling at start

The more something threatens your identity more you will avoid doing it

  1. release phase

  2. flow

Do what you love Flow healthy life

  1. Recovery

Embrace struggle

+Optimal learning strategy

80/20 20 percentage of action leads to 80percent result

Parkinson's law Works expands to fill the time given

Set Tight deadlines are necessary to get best out of you

Blooms taxonomy School learning system is failing

Creating - is the most efficient way to learn something followed by Evaluating, Analyzing, Applying, Understanding Remember (passive Learning, bad approach)

Behavioursm Surrounding yourself with talented and knowledgeable person

Conitivismks Find connection between what you know and new learning helps strengthen neuro transmitters Develop your mindset, common people think they can't be good at maths, but its not correct Keep reading and learning makes you strong

Constructivism Passive reading and listening to video is not efficient way of learning, create something out of your learning : most impotant - start with complex problem and on the way to resolve that learn basics

+Optimal Learning Techniques

Retrieval Practice Distributed practice Reasearch has shown that when learning is distributed uniformly across week brings more learning. You need to space out your practice session across days intead of all at once in one day Practice test Reasearch shows taking practice test aftrer chapters is more effective than revising Also you can find a study partner and ask questions to each other.

Inter leaved practice Mixing several types of items while practicing . Block practicing - example in badminton if you try different types of serve instead of just doing one service at time brings better results in long term Elaborative intervention Ask why intead of what The more learning hooks you create more space will form for new learning

Self explanattion Explain to yourself with efforts. So that brai. Can remember it.explain to urself as if prac

Lifetayle Improve sleep quality

Excerise Balances neurotransmitter Increase brain volume Smoking= sitting Walking+ audiobooks great combination

Fasting Produces energy Improve memory It takes a while for body to adapylt 5:2 fasting - eat 300k cals on 2 days of fasting,

Meditation It physically change brain structure It Increase grey matter IncreAses volume of cerebral 8 weeks of daily meditation enhance brain size Stress in induced by own though- Meditation helps Placebo effect. Attention and working memory increased in 12 weeks of meditation

#learnings
